description: A14FJ - Day 3, part 2: TV and LM Housekeeping

tokens: The crew of Apollo 14 is busy with checks in the Lunar Module's primary systems in preparation for their upcoming Lunar Orbit Insertion manoeuvre the next day.

An unprecedented manoeuvre earlier during the day has adjusted the onboard clock and Ground Elapsed Time to take into account the 40-minute launch delay that left their timeline out of synch with the pre-printed Flight Plan.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Editor's note:All transcript times are presented according to the GET update at 054:53:36 that saw the mission timer moved forward 40 minutes, 2.90 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We're in the process of pressurizing theLMnow, as you can probably see.
We're holding for the integrity check at this point.
I have the television set up and I can turn it on at your command.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Stand by on the TV, we'll see if they're ready.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: They're all set up, Ed; you can turn it on any time.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: You should be able to recognize it.
There's its field of view at the moment.
And Alan is up in the tunnel working the pressurization procedure.
Stuart is coming up from under the Commander's seat - you can see his head starting to come into the field of view now - from stowing items down under the seat.
Let's see if I can get a little better picture here for you.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Stu's head looks pretty good in color there.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: You mean that color wheel can handle red, Freddo?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: The Apollo TV camera produces a color image by rotating a disc with red, blue and green filters spinning in front of the vidicon imaging tube.
The resulting 60-fields-per-second signal therefore represents sequential analyses of the scene in those three colours.
The color information is reconstructed from the video signal through a lengthy process back on Earth after the basic signal has been retrieved and corrected for timing.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: It seems to be doing a pretty good job there.
I noticed some speckles on the window there.
Is that - did y'all end up with a little debris on there?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: We have a few ice crystals, apparently, that have adhered to it from the various dumps we've made.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: You know, Freddo, that window was clean as all get out until we started all the dumping.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: We've just opened up the pressurization valve between the two spacecraft.
And we're flowing into theLMfrom the Command Module.
Give you a picture if you can see it here.
There's our cabin pressure gage, shows your quantity as well.
Let's see if I can get it in view.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: A little dark and out of focus there right now, Ed.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: That's a little better, I can now see the - your little card there showing the antenna patterns.
But the needles on the gage is a little hard to read.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: We'll give up on that, Freddo.
Our pressure is equal between the Command Module and theLM, and we will start removing tunnel hardware at this point.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: And, Houston, we're starting to shoot up into the tunnel here where - Alan Shepard is starting to pull the hatch out - if I can get him to look down for a minute and say hello to the world.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: I can see a form there that I guess is Al.
It's - this picture is just a little bit dark.
You can see the lights up in the tunnel.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: I think we'll have just a little bit more light once this hatch gets out of the way.
We have all of our floodlights on full right now.
While they're doing that, let's slide out to theCDR's window and take a look at the Moon, which happens to be right out the window.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Going to coach me a little bit, Fred, as I approach this?
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Okay, we can see it now; it's right down in the lower right quadrant of the window.
The picture, though, we're seeing is a crescent, and it's really not showing too much of the detail, Ed.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Assume you're zooming the camera now, Ed.
And we lost the picture of the Moon.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: I think what happened when you zoomed, Ed, you ended up pointing above it.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Do you see anything now, Freddo?
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Okay, it just disappeared out the lower right corner.
We had it there for a little while.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: There, it barely came in the bottom portion of the picture now.
Is that about Tranquility, somewhere in there ?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: It's past Tranquility, and I think it's - it's approaching zero longitude at this point, I believe.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: A few hours ago we just passed Delta 'Tegnius [?], Fred.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Stu is most likely referring to the crater Albategnius as a landmark here.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: It - I was looking through the sextant there just a minute ago.
It - all of Descartes - around Descartes and the Highlands showed up real well and the terminator had just moved on over just past Platte [?].
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: And for your information, in the audience in the back here, we have most of the family in tonight - this morning down here, I should say.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: 3:15 is a good time of night to have a TV show.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Yes, that's about right in the center of the picture now.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: I've got full zoom on it, Freddo.
Let's see if I can darken it.
It'll improve the picture a little.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: It's so bright, Ed, we're - we have the crescent, but not too much detail on.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: And from our point of view, the Moon appears about the size of a grapefruit, held at the arm's length.
It's going to get considerably bigger, and if I don't have the Earth to compare it with at the moment.
But I suspect they are about the same size or maybe the Moon is starting to exceed the Earth in apparent size, [Long pause]
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: And the big board now has you at ...
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Okay, I'm going to come back inside.
We have now stowed the hatch.
And Stu and Al are up starting to bring the probe out.
And very soon we'll be able to make the trip into theLMwith the camera and see what we can find in there.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: We have not, as you can obviously see, rehearsed this.
Our procedures are new to us, going into theLMfor the first time.
And, as a matter of fact, we were a little bit rushed getting the pressurization procedure complete in order to get in on time.
We'll be ready here in about 2 minutes to move on into theLM, I think.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: And, now, let's go back inside the Command Module.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Are we looking up in the tunnel area now, Ed?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Yes, I'll be bringing the probe out ...
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Yes, I'm trying to, Fred, but it's dark.
Just 1 second, I'll see if I can improve the light situation for us.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: You're looking directly up into the tunnel now.
The probe has been released from the drogue, and Stu will be bringing it straight down through the tunnel.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: We got that picture in pretty good now.
We can see it coming on down.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Al and Stu are taking the probe down under the right-hand couch now.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Yes, we can see Al in the picture now.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: He's probably clearer for you than he is on our monitor.
It's a little dark in here.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: You can look up the tunnel and see the drogue [Garbled], Ed, and I'll go and pick it up for you.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: You're looking directly at the drogue now, which is gray in color and which has a hole in the center above the capture latches.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: In our color pictures here, the opening in the drogue looks red in color.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: And Alan has it out and he's starting to come down the tunnel with it.
That's probably a little dark for you, but he's coming down anyhow.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Okay, and we've temporarily, I hope, lost our picture down here, Ed.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Fred, do you have your picture back yet?
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: And 14, the problem with the loss of picture is on our end of the street.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Tell me when you have it back and we're ready to go through into theLM.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: You may continue the commentary, I guess.
It's being received out at the site and being recorded; we're just not getting it - plumbed into here.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: While we were just holding up here, Alan has slipped on through into theLMand is opening up the window shades and turning on the lights, so that as we go in, we will have some lights to give you a little better picture.
I'm waiting here until - to get your picture back before I start in; however, if you would like, I'll go ahead and go into theLM.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: They don't have an estimate right now.
We might wait a couple of minutes; and then, I guess, if we haven't got it back, you can proceed.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Okay, Alan's coming back through the tunnel now and it will be clear here in a minute.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Houston, while we're waiting for your picture to come back and, since it is being recorded at the site, we will take a quick station break and let Alan give us a commercial for Apollo 14.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: As you can see, we're now right on schedule.
And it looks as though the first midcourse correction is going to put us in for the lunar orbit insertion burn - very close.
We're - we're currently preparing to go into theLMas Ed has told you; and, so far, everything is working very well in the Command Module.
We're very pleased with the way the systems are working.
Everything is quiet, going along extremely smoothly, and we have a happy little ship here.
We're adapting to weightlessness, I think, very rapidly.
And everything is going along very smoothly at this point.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: And we have a picture back now.
It's just a little bit on the dark side, but we can see you quite well.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Hey, that's a good picture, Ed.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Why don't you show the docking latches while you go by, Ed.
See how they look on the television.
Can you see the docking latches, Freddo?
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: We can see on down to the tunnel and the top of the ascent engine cover.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: I think it's significant that, in spite of our problem the other day, that when Stu - Stu finally got us into the docking ring that all of them snapped shut, which is - was very well lined up.
And our docking index, as you know, cost us a case of beer, I think, because it's within 1 degree.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: And you might let us know, Ed, how you find the fit through the tunnel there.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Hey, was that question from Joe?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: I'm not sure he'd own up to it.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: I'm through the tunnel, and I'm right at the top of theLM.
I'm shining the - showing the camera down on the main console on the Commander's side.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: I'll go ahead and go on down and twist around, and maybe we can look out the window.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Yes, we see what looks to be a patch there, Ed.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Is it good enough for you to read it?
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Is it something like beep, beep or -
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Must be some prompting from the ground.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: And, Ed, a good portion of your swing here, the picture is a little bit on the dark side.
You might try opening it up just a little bit more.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: I've had it open all the way, Freddo.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Well, I guess you can't do any better than that.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Did you find theLMpretty clean, Ed, when y'all first came aboard or was anything loose in there?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: No, both spacecraft, Freddo, have been immaculate.
I think we have found one washer floating along about 1 day ago, and we have seen nothing that was foreign to the spacecraft either before or since that time.
And, as I say that, I see one floating by me right now.
It may be the same washer.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Yes, I'd like to point out that Ed is talking about something external to the spacecraft.
The cabin above the Command Module and theLMare extremely clean.
We've been very pleased with the way they've looked.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Yes, I think from what we've heard along the line, we actually expected maybe to see a few more nuts and bolts.
I think a lot of credit goes to checkout crew and everything because this spacecraft is really in good shape.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: And, Freddo, I'm passing the camera back to Alan in the Command Module.
We don't have enough light in here to really give you a good picture.
And there's not much help from the outside since the Sun is behind us right now.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: I - we got a pretty good picture of you down in the tunnel right now.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Did you hear that last comment I made about the crew and how clean the spacecraft was?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Well, that was planted, you know, for all the authorized people that worked on the spacecraft.
You know, we're really unindated [sic] with unauthorized objects in both spacecraft.
I think Ed was showing you one up there, but if you could see this.
I don't know if any of the backup crew is down in there tonight or not.
But - okay, how about - about here - [Garbled] But, they've left their calling card.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Okay, we have a pretty good picture of that, Stu.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Tell them we sure appreciate every compartment that we open up having one of these come floating out of it.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Stu, you want to take the camera?
Perhaps you can move it on up to the window.
I don't know how much light there is, but sure is a good shot of the Moon out there.
You might try again with the monitor so you can see what it looks like to them.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Well, we'll give it a go.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Okay, we can see the Moon again in the number - number 1 window.
It's down in the lower left corner now, Stu.
Hey, that's about right in the center.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Okay, that's - the picture's a little distorted now, Stu.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Okay, that ought to help it a little.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: It's still hard to see in this picture - much in the way of detail of the features - any features on the surface.
Right at the terminator, we can pick up a few craters; but, other than that, it's really hard to see very much.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Yes, I didn't think it was going to show up too much there, Fred, because that's really the way it looks with the eye.
It's just now starting, you know, to get into the area where the larger craters would show up.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Okay, we've got a good picture of Al there, now.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Okay, he's got the transfer items that he's taking up into theLMfor the activation, and he might just give you a word on what they are.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: The purpose of the excursion into theLMat this time is to check out some of the communications, do a little housekeeping, look - generally look the vehicle over, and also, in this particular case, we are going to take some pictures of the Command Module while we're there.
This is done during the flight on the way to the Moon to save time, so that when we actually go into theLMfor the final time prior to the descent, there will be less things to do.
So, I have a package of things here in my hand which I'll now be taking up into theLMand will proceed on with our housekeeping tasks up there.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Okay, we can see him heading out with the package in hand there.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Okay, is there enough light up in there, Fred?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Looks like running an obstacle course in the early going, getting by all the hoses.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: And, Freddo, I'm starting back through the tunnel now to pick up the rest of the equipment.
We have several 16- and 70-millimeter camera Mags that are being transferred over.
We'll pick those up and be right back with you.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Okay, I guess the picture I'm looking at now, as you're pointing back into theLEBin the area where the optics would be.
And I can see the radiation meter back there.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: That's affirmative, Fred; I'm just really trying to get the camera out of the way there for a minute.
Ed's got the other film magazine.
He's headed back up into theLMnow.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Yes, Ed fits through there quite easily.
I guess zero g really does help.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: If you're commenting on what I think you're commenting on, it was totally uncalled for, Freddo.
And, Houston, we are both - Al and I are both in theLMnow with all of our transfer items, and we will proceed to go ahead and give it a checkout as per the time line.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: I'll try to move up here, and maybe we can look over the shoulder a little bit.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: I can see a moving back and forth; I guess your head is down through now.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Okay, we're going to get a picture of it all up here, Fred.
I'm up ahead by theLMhatch now, trying to watch them work in theLM; but it doesn't look like it's going to be bright enough.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: It's - it's a pretty dark picture now, Stu.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Yes, I'm afraid that's not going to show up.
If we had a little light, I guess we need a little Sun through theLMwindow.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Well, Houston, I guess Fred and I'll have to give you a show from - I mean Stu and I'll have to give you a show from that side.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: And you're cutting in and out there, Ed.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: As you know, we have the probe and drogue out.
Is there anybody that - who's interested in taking a look at those, or are you pretty well satisfied that just taking still - still pictures of it from here on out will do the trick?
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: The word is the pictures should suffice.
I - They're not that - particularly interested in another look right now.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Is there anything else you'd like to see before we sign out for the evening?
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: The - I guess the answer is no to your last question.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: In that case, we'll sign off from Apollo 14.
We appreciate the opportunity to show you around the spacecraft and a little bit of theLM; and I hope that next time you see theLM, it'll have more light, so you'll be able to see it better.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 61 hours 23 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
That ends about 42 minutes of television time from Apollo 14.
Beginning of theLMcheckout, the checkout of the Lunar Module some of the housekeeping chores that the crew have to do for preparation for manningLMon landing day.
The guests back in the viewing room for the TV transmission included Mrs.
Laura Snider, a nephew, Bob Williams.
Russell Brewer and a friend of Mrs.
Others in the viewing room were Mrs.
Louise Mitchell, wife of the Lunar Module Pilot, their daughters Carol, Collin, and Libby and Ed's mother Mrs.
Stu Roosa's wife Joan, their children Chris, Jack, Allen, and Rosemary.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Okay, Freddo, do you want me to stay on the High Gain here or go to Omni Charlie?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: They would like you to stay on High Gain.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Okay; and, Stu, would you pass one word on down to Al and Ed?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Would you tell them to - to give us a call before they proceed on page 1-15, where they're going to turn on the - the comm and get a Go from us and make sure we've got good lock on theS-IVB.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Freddo, I'm still on the comm.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Okay, and I guess when you get there, Ed, just check with us, and - and I'll make sure they're set up good on the IU before you press on.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: This Apollo Control continuing with the roster of guests and parents who are in the viewing room during the television pass.
Two of Stu Roosa's sisters, Mrs.
Gloria Sessums and a close family friend, Mr.
The space digital display now showing distance and velocity reference to the Moon.
Now showing 44 175 nautical miles [81,812 km] out from the Moon, velocity toward the Moon 3 241 feet per second [988 m/s].
At 61 hours 27 minutes Ground Elapsed Time, still live on air-ground during the checkout of the Lunar Module, this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Just wanted to see if you were still around.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Okay, Freddo, I guess - sitting here looking, the next hour - we - You'd originally said we're going to go intoPTCat 62:10.
I don't know if that was before you came up with the waste water dump or not; we don't really want to start that before the waste water dump, do we?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: The time - Let's see, I show on the flight-plan change here is 63:10, establishPTC.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Okay, Stu; but, in any case, you're right, not before the water dump.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Yes, that - that was my mistake.
I had marked that in and before I'd moved everything up an hour, and I've got it back over there in the right place, and I did mark it off.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: And, 14, while we're talking aboutPTC, Stu, I'd like B/D roll selected before you crank it up.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: You got any idea, Stu, where they are in the Good Book, so we can maybe begin and be warned about the IU business?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Houston, the - the ED Bus Bat A is reading 37 volts; Bat B, 37 volts.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Yes, I need you to turn on your ...
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Hey, Fred, when you get a minute, I need a VHF A check.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: And we'd like high taps on theLM.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: We're going to get them to them.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: I guess we're reading Antares now, loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Do you read loud and clear?
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Too many of you are speaking at once there.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: I was just giving you an estimated check from Antares.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Loud and clear, and go ahead, Kitty Hawk.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Your first one is 20 seconds at 24 frames a second, and then 20 seconds at 1 frame per second.
Then, when you get near the end, when you're going to shut it off, you want 10 seconds at 24 frames per second, and then 1 frame per second as it peters off.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Except they didn't want to run it any longer than 180 seconds, which is pretty close to 3 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: And, Ed, or Antares, just a reminder.
You're on SPA Down Noise Backup now, so you're hot-mike.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: We're holding up, Freddo, on our procedures until Al finishes getting the waste water dump pictures.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Houston, from theLM, this water dump looks like a snow storm.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: That's the way I figured it would look.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Fred, were you getting your LO bit rate?
If so, I'll switch you over to Hi.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: LO bit rate, you can go to step 3.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: We got Hi bit rate now, Ed.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Houston, how do you read Antares?
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: I read you loud and clear, Ed.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Antares, we're reading you loud and clear on LO bit rate, now.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Okay, it's not bad at all.
I'd say you're loud and clear, also.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Kitty Hawk, Antares, how do you read VHF A?
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Ed, how do you read on A?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: How do you read VHF A, now?
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Okay, I guess I'm ready for step 8, now.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Read you loud and clear, Ed.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Stand by - We're still having trouble - not getting our VHF turned up.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: How do you read me on VHF B?
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: How do you read VHF B?
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: We'd like a readout on Bat 5 and 6 - voltage.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: We're ready to dispense with the water dump, now.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: I've already shut it off, Freddo.
I'm just coming back up, now.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: How do you read on A, Ed?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Read you loud and clear on A.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Bats 5 and 6 are reading 36 and a half and 37 volts, respectively.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Can I turn - Do you want me to turn the VHF Off?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: We're going to skip theOPScheckout until Al gets through with his camera work.
And I'm going to go ahead with the comm deactivation.
We'll pick the checkout up shortly.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Antares is going off the air for a couple of days.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: With all their checks done, Ed and Al can switch the LM comms off and continue with their checks.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We just transferred to - we just transferred to Command Module power.
Be coming back up in a minute.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: I got - I got a question there.
I was wondering if Ed recalls back on step 1, on comm activation, if he had to switch the antenna - S-Band Antenna switch, was it already in Aft or did he move it to Forward and then back to Aft?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Okay, this is Antares talking through Kitty Hawk's transmitters.
We found the S-Band Antenna switch in Aft as the checklist called for, and he did not move in for step 1.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 63 hours 8 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
Presently crew of Apollo 14, two of them that is, en route back to the Command Module if indeed they are not already in with the tunnel closed.
Change of shift hand-over taking place in the control room now where Pete Franks' Orange Team is taking over from the Gold Team headed up by Flight Director Gerry Griffin.
We will have the change of shift press conference in the Houston news center within the next half hour or so.
And, at 63 hours 9 minutes Ground Elapsed Time, this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Okay, Al, are you back in the Kitty Hawk, now?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: No, I'm still in Antares, but I'm using Kitty Hawk cable.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: The question is, we've got you back on - Antares - back on Command Module power, and they're showing about 2 amps high.
And, the question is, has the reconfiguration been complete as per the initial activation status chart for circuit breakers back on 13 and 14 yet?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: The source pressure on theCDR's reading 6200; lowest pressure onLMPis reading 6000.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: That's Al Shepard talking to Fred Haise out here in Mission Control.
Meanwhile in Mission Control, we're pressing on with our handover between shifts.
The Orange Team coming in now to replace the Gold Team.
We're at 63 hours 15 minutes into the flight.
We presently show Apollo 14 traveling at a speed of 2500 to 54 feet per second [?].
And, at a distance away from Earth of 179 900 nautical miles [333,175 km].
Continuing to monitor this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We rechecked our circuit breaker configuration, and we found thatEPSDC Bus Volt, panel 11, was out.

tokens: I was just looking through the checklist on page 1-18; under the 64-hour callout, it shows thatEPSDC Bus Volt circuit breaker back open.
It comes after you check the circuit breaker charge, up at the top of the page.
So I guess that DC Volt should be open.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Gordon Fullerton now on the CapCom position replacing Fred Haise.
That was Fullerton speaking just now to Commander Al Shepard.
We're 63 hours 26 minutes, Apollo 14 180 184 nautical miles [333,700 km] away from the Earth.
And, traveling at a speed of 2546 feet per second [776 m/s].

tokens: I think our checklist is finally in phase with your checklist, and the DC Bus Volt circuit breaker, panel 11, is out.

tokens: Participants for the change of shift news conference are presently en route.

tokens: That current in theLMseems to be dropping down.

transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: As I say, I think we're finally in phase now.
We - The - the last item we did was turn out the floodlights which probably helps the situation.

tokens: If - if it's convenient to get to, we'd like to know how much of that magazine Foxtrot you used taking pictures of the water dump.

tokens: 63 hours 37 minutes now into the flight.
Al Shepard, Ed Mitchell back in the Kitty Hawk now.

We show Apollo 14 at 180,438 nautical miles [334,171 km] and traveling at a speed of 2,540 feet per second [774 m/s].
At 63 hours 37 minutes into the flight, this is Apollo Control, Houston.

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston at 63 hours 39 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
The change of shift news conference is scheduled to start momentarily and at this point we will take down the line and tape any conversations which might transpire.

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston, at 63 hours 59 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
We presently show Apollo 14 at the distance of 180,983 nautical miles [335,181 km] away from Earth, traveling at a speed of 2,526 feet per second [770 m/s].
CapCom Gordon Fullerton has had limited conversation with Apollo 14 since we had taken the line down for the change of shift conference.

tokens: Down a little, we'll take a look and see how your rates look.

tokens: You look good now; you're clear to spin it up.

tokens: Also, we'd like Omni Bravo, and you're clear to secure the high gain antenna.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: As you heard CapCom Fullerton talking both to Stu Roosa and Ed Mitchell.
Apollo 14 has now returned to its slow rotation, the passive thermal control of the spacecraft, and of the high gain antenna has been secured.
We're 64 hours 1 minute into the flight.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're watching yourPTC, and it doesn't look too good.
It's heading right on out of the box.
We lost data just as you started to spin up with that antenna switchover.
It was P00r timing on our part.
So we're not sure just how the start went, but we're sure this one isn't going to work.
So, at your convenience, if you'll restart another - or redamp the rates and stand by for our Go for another startup.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Gordon Fullerton advising Ed Mitchell to - that it's our consideration to reestablish the passive thermal control mode.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: If you stop near a roll angle of either zero or 180, that will give us better high bit rate to watch your rates.

tokens: Stu's coming up to the top now.

tokens: 14 now 181,390 nautical miles [335,934 km] away from Earth.

tokens: It just headed right on out to the limit for some reason, Stu.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: So that one didn't take, huh?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: It went right straight on out toward a limit on our plot here.
We didn't get a good read-out of the - of the initiation of it, because we had that antenna switchover right at the time.
I'll stand by until we get through this - into the next Omni here.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: I mentioned to Ed if you'll stop the roll on near zero or near 180, we'll have good antenna angle for high bit rate and can watch the rates that way.

tokens: This is Apollo Control Houston at 64 hours, 48 minutesGET.
We presently show Apollo 14 at a distance of 182,151 nautical miles [337,344 km] out from Earth, and travelling at a speed of 2,496 feet per second [761 m/s].

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston, 65 hours and 30 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
We now show Apollo 14 at a distance of 182,520 nautical miles [338,027 km] traveling at a speed of 2,487 feet per second [758 m/s].
We'll continue to monitor for any conversation as it should come up between CapCom Gordon Fullerton here in Mission Control and members of the Apollo 14 crew.

tokens: Judging by how far the spacecraft has flown since the last report from the Public Affairs Officer, he probably meant to say "65 hours and 3 minutes".

tokens: As you're sitting there, your high gain is pretty well pointed at us.
We'd like to bring it up to watch the start of this next attempt at PC -PTC.
Would you go to Auto - and then select High Gain?

tokens: Okay, Gordon, it looks like we've got the High Gain.

tokens: That's Stu Roosa aboard Apollo 14 talking to CapCom Gordon Fullerton.

tokens: You have a Go to start the spinup.

tokens: Apollo Control, Houston, 65 hours, 08 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
Apollo 14 182,644 nautical miles [338,256 km] away from Earth presently traveling at a speed of 2,484 feet per second [757 m/s].
That was Gordon Fullerton giving a Go for restart of the passive thermal control.

tokens: It looks to us like you might have missed one jet enable, Delta-2.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Your rates are still good, if you want to give her a start.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We're showing the O2flow up a little bit to eight-tenths and suggest you might look around at the - all the overboard drains, valves, and make sure they're tightly secured.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Give us Omni Bravo, and you're clear to secure the high gain antenna.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We see that the cabin pressure, which was down to near 5, is back up to 5 dec - 5.1, and the flow rate is decreasing now.
Does this help you - give you any clues as to what the problem might have been?
Did you find any loose valves?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We're having a problem with a sticky Myrtle.
We're working [Garbled] equipment problem right now.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: We presently show 14 at a distance of 183 025 nautical miles [338,962 km] away from Earth and traveling at a velocity of 2475 feet per second [754 m/s].
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: I have a short shopping list of items for you here.
First of all, the state vector on board there is in good shape and no update will be required as scheduled at 66 hours.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: And it's looking like midcourse 4 will be about 3.5 feet per second - 3-1/2 feet per second.
And one question, on the lens - the 18-millimeter lens that you took over to use to take the pictures of the water dump.
Just to help us keep track of where things are, did you bring that back and restow it in the Command Module?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: The lens is back and stowed in the Command Module, and understand midcourse 4 will be about 3.2.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Stu, that's 3.5, but that's close enough as - as close as we can tell now.
It's hanging in there and, well, maybe its too early to predict; it looks like it's going to take this time.
There is some - an O2heater reconfiguration shown at 65.10 in the Flight Plan to do and the presleep items on the checklist, and that's all we have before you retire for the day.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We just, about 10 minutes ago, configured the heaters, and we're going to press into the presleep here momentarily.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: That was CapCom, Gordon Fullerton talking to Command Module Pilot Stu Roosa.
The report he made indicated that no alignment of the platform would be required before the start of the rest period.
Also the plots of the passive thermal control mode as displayed on the guidance and control officers console here at Mission Control appear very favorable.
Midcourse correction 4, flight dynamics advises presently looks like a 3.5 foot per second burn.

This is presently scheduled at a Ground Elapsed Time of 77 hours, 38 minutes and we now show Apollo 14 at a distance of 183,490 nautical miles [339,823 km], traveling at a velocity of 2463 feet per second [751 m/s].
We're at 65 hours, 44 minutes Ground Elapsed Time and this is Apollo Control, Houston.

tokens: This is Apollo Control Houston at 65 hours 55 minutesGET.
Apollo 14 now at a distance of 183 764 nautical miles [340,331 km] away from the Earth.
Velocity now showing 2456 feet per second [749 m/s].
The crew of Apollo 14 just about ready to start their rest period, we may - or may not - have further contact with them.
We will standby and continue to monitor in the eventuality we do have contact with the crew prior to the start of their rest.
We're at 65 hours 56 minutesGETand this is Apollo Control Houston.

tokens: I'm ready for an E-memory dump.
Can you take it with this comm?

tokens: I'm reading you just barely, Stu.
I'll check if we can take the E-MOD in this kind of antenna configuration.

tokens: Stu, we're going to have to wait a couple of minutes until a better antenna gets up.

tokens: And, Gordon, I've got some onboard read-outs when you're ready.

tokens: And, Gordon, for a crew status, we're all in good shape and no medication.

tokens: Apollo Control, Houston, 66 hours 03 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.

tokens: Looks like we've got the dump complete.

He just wanted a confirmation that you had.

tokens: Following that conversation between CapCom Gordon Fullerton and Command Module Pilot, Stu Roosa, we expect the crew to start their rest period, the E-memory dump completed, then this should close out our contact with the crew.
We now show Apollo 14 at 184 033 nautical miles [340,829 km] away from Earth and traveling at a speed of 2450 feet per second [747 m/s].
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Houston, 14 is signing off for the evening.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: That was space craft commander Al Shepard advising the mission control in Houston that the crew of Apollo 14 was ready to get some sleep.
We now show Apollo 14 at 184,292 nautical miles [341,309 km] out from Earth.
Velocity now reads 2443 feet per second [745 m/s].GETtime presently at 66 hours 18 minutes.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston, at 66 hours, 35 minutes now into the flight.
Our displays in mission control show Apollo 14 at a distance of 184,700 nautical miles [342,064 km] away from the Earth.
Traveling at a velocity of 2433 feet per second [742 m/s].
Since the crew of Apollo 14 has begun the Flight Plan sleep period we will bring down the line at this time and pick up again if any conversation should transpire.
We're at 66 hours, 36 minutes Ground Elapsed Time and this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control Houston at 66 - 67 hours 7 minutesGET.
We expect CapCom Gordon Fullerton will place a call to the spacecraft.
Here we're showing a high O2 flow rate.
Apparently, most probably caused by a leak of one of the over board vents.
If this call is placed, Fullerton will ask one of the crew members to - to check all the valves on board.
There's some consideration that they might not be seated properly.
The passive thermal control mode however still remains stable.
We'll standby and continue to monitor in the event that call is placed, the line is now up live.
Since Apollo 14 has passed through lunar sphere influence at 66 hours 49 minutes 4 seconds, our displays in Mission Control are now referenced to the Moon.
We show Apollo 14 now presently 33 202 nautical miles [61,490 km] away from the Moon, and traveling at a speed of 3,306 feet per second [1,008 m/s], relative to the Moon.
We'll standby and see if the call is placed to Apollo 14.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: The shift to Moon-centric rather than Earth-centric reporting of their distance is not merely a convenience.
This also affects the guidance system, which either works in the Earth or the lunar sphere of influence, using the center of mass of each body as the starting point.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: In practical terms, Apollo 14 has escaped the gravitational pull of the Earth that has been slowing it down since their Translunar injection burn, and they have now started to fall towards the Moon at an increasing speed.
They have already gained 266 m/s of velocity from lunar gravitational pull since the shift.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Sorry to disturb you, but we have been watching some parameters on theECS.

We watched the cabin leak down to 5.1 the last we saw and we've also noticed a - that the main Regs have opened as a result, and your O2flow has been steadily increasing; last time we had high bit rate, it was about 4/10ths of a pound per hour.
ThePTCis being disturbed, but it still - is stable.
We wanted to alert you to try to troubleshoot this leak before we throw thePTCout of bounds.
What we'd like you to do is check all the overboard drain valves, one at a time.

And, this time, we would like to try to tie down precisely which - which valve is the guilty one and so we would like - as you either tighten them or jiggle them or whatever you can think of - to do to the valves, do each one individually and give us time to watch the flow rate and see if we can tie it down.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: That's Ed Mitchell responding to that call.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Houston, the Waste Management Dump valve is going to Off at this point.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Gordon, did you notice a step increase of the flow rate, or has it just been a gradually increasing thing over the last hour or so?
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: It's been a gradual increase; however, just - We've been watching the data after you told us you closed the Waste Management Overboard Drain, and we're seeing it drop down.
That may be the culprit right there.
Can you verify the configuration that you were in?
Did you have the cap on the Myrtle; was that closed?
And was the Waste Management Overboard Drain open?
Prior to our waking you up?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: This is Apollo Control Houston, we will standby while we're having a switching of antennas.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We missed your answer to my question completely during the Omni switchover there.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Your last question about the configuration of the waste management drain to the Myrtle?
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: I guess - the Myrtle - the waste management system had been in use several times for the last hour, and if you saw changes probably fluctuating up and down, it was undoubtedly due to that system.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: That's - I mean - that's since - since you signed out for the night, is that right?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Right now the drain is closed, and it had been closed, reopened, and closed, at least twice since we signed off for the night.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: The O2flow has - dropped back down now.
It's not come down; it - it still appears that the main Regs may be flowing a little bit.
What we suggest is that you use the Direct O2to pump the cabin back up to 5.7, and then go on back to sleep and we'll try not to bother you.
ThePTClooks like it should hold okay through the sleep period.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: You heard that last conversation between CapCom Gordon Fullerton and Lunar Module Pilot Ed Mitchell.
It would appear that the use of the waste management system may have been the culprit in this case.
We now show Apollo 14 at 32,766 nautical miles [60,683 km] away from the Moon and traveling at a speed of 3,309 feet per second [1,009 m/s].
That speed relative to the Moon.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: First of all, Al's got a sensor replaced.
He wanted the medics to take a look at it, and pump the cabin up, shut off the Direct O2, and that flow is down to 2/10ths now.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: 14, Houston; we're going to have to wait until we rotate around to an - an antenna angle that we can get High Bit Rate before we can check that sensor, but we'll give you a call in a minute or two when we're ready.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: 14, Houston.CDR's biomed data looks very good, according to Dr.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Charles "Chuck" Berry was the Medical Director and had the overall responsibility for crew health.
An omnipresent figure since the selection of the first Mercury astronauts, he was known to everyone.
Berry is present in Mission Control and overseeing the incoming electrocardiogram display.
Normally the SURGEON console was manned around the clock by one of the doctors assignnd to the mission - in the case of Apollo 14, Drs.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I just changed the paste in the sensor and put a new sticky washer on the electrode [Garbled.]
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: They're - they're willing to sign you up for a permanent job at doing that, if you'd like to after the flight.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: If you're satisfied then, I'll return my comm configuration to the sleep configuration.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Let me make one quick check here.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Well, I guess that's all we've got.
We'll say good night once again.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We show Apollo 14 at 32,470 nautical miles [60,134 km] away from the Moon and now traveling at a speed of 3,311 feet per second [1,009 m/s] relative to the Moon.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston, at 68 hours 04 minutes into the flight - Ground Elapsed Time.
We presently show Apollo 14 at a distance of 31,392 nautical miles [58,138 km] away from the Moon and traveling at a speed of 3321 feet per second [1,012 m/s] relative to the Moon.
We've had no further conversation with the crew of Apollo 14 since they were awakened by CapCom Gordon Fullerton to check a high O2flow rate.
Presumably the crew, if not yet sleeping, shortly will be.
We're at 68 hours, 05 minutes into the flight and this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: At 68 hours, 33 minutes now into the flight of Apollo 14.
We presently show Apollo 14 at a distance of 30,431 nautical miles [56,358 km] away from the Moon, now traveling at a velocity of 3,329 feet per second [1,015 m/s].
We've had no communications with the crew.
Since our last report, the crew now in its rest period - they are in their rest period.
Meanwhile, activity in Mission Control has been conversations over the flight directors loop have been quite subdued.
It's been mainly a scene where flight controllers are studying their displays, they are considering the activities that will lie ahead on the next shift.
A test of one of the lunar modular batteries by spacecraft Commander, Al Shepard, and Ed Mitchell is being considered either at the time ofLMactivation or before.
Ascent battery number 5 has shown a reading of 3 tenths of a volt low on the open circuit voltage.

If the test is run, it would involve putting theLMbattery number 5, the one under question, on one of the busses for an independent or second reading off another transducer and provide a second data point.
The present reading is taken from the battery transducer without any load.
If the second reading indicates a shift in signal conditioner then it would indicate faulty instrumentation rather than a fault of the battery itself.
We will stand by and continue to monitor this possibility and provide updates as they become available.
We're at 68 hours, 35 minutes Ground Elapsed Time and this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston, at 69 hours and 09 minutes now into the flight of Apollo 14.
We presently show Apollo 14 at a distance of 29,256 nautical miles [54,182 km] away from the Moon traveling at a velocity of 3,340 feet per second [1,018 m/s].
We've had no communication with the crew of Apollo 14 since our last report.
Our clock at Mission Control shows a projected wake-up time of 5 hours and 50 minutes from this time.
At this time, we will take down the release line and bring it back should we have contact with the crew, which is not presently anticipated.
At 69 hours, 10 minutes Ground Elapsed Time, this is Apollo Control, Houston.
elapsed time, this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO
